h1Title: Amenorrhea: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Amenorrhea is the absence of menstrual periods — defined as no menarche by age 15 (primary) or the absence of three or more consecutive periods in a woman who has previously menstruated (secondary). Secondary amenorrhea affects roughly 3-5% of reproductive-age women at any given time, excluding pregnancy, which is the single most common cause and must always be excluded first. The hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis controls the menstrual cycle, and disruption at any point — from stress and low body weight to pituitary tumors and primary ovarian insufficiency — can halt menstruation. Beyond missed periods, amenorrhea signals estrogen deficiency that accelerates bone loss, impairs fertility, and raises cardiovascular risk. Most causes are reversible when identified promptly; treatment targets the underlying condition.

keyStats.prevalence: Secondary amenorrhea affects 3-5% of reproductive-age women; primary amenorrhea affects 1 in 2,500 girls (Master-Hunter & Heiman, Am Fam Physician 2006)
keyStats.demographics: Secondary amenorrhea is most common in adolescents and women aged 18-40; hypothalamic amenorrhea disproportionately affects female athletes and women with low BMI; primary ovarian insufficiency affects 1% of women under 40
keyStats.avgAge: Primary amenorrhea evaluated at age 15-16; secondary amenorrhea can occur at any reproductive age; premature ovarian insufficiency presents before age 40
keyStats.globalCases: Amenorrhea is the presenting complaint in approximately 5% of all gynecology outpatient visits globally

definition: Amenorrhea (ICD-10: N91.2 — Amenorrhea, unspecified) is the absence or cessation of menstrual bleeding. Primary amenorrhea is defined as the failure to achieve menarche by age 15 years in the presence of normal secondary sexual development, or by age 13 in the absence of breast development. Secondary amenorrhea is defined as the cessation of previously regular menses for 3 or more consecutive months, or the loss of previously irregular menses for 6 or more months. Physiologic amenorrhea — during pregnancy, lactation, and after menopause — is normal and should not be pathologically classified. Pathologic amenorrhea results from disorders at four anatomical levels: the outflow tract (uterus, cervix, vagina), the ovaries, the anterior pituitary, and the hypothalamus, as well as from systemic endocrinopathies including thyroid disease and hyperprolactinemia. The condition is managed by gynecologists and reproductive endocrinologists, often in collaboration with endocrinologists. Amenorrhea has implications beyond fertility: estrogen deficiency from any cause — whether from hypothalamic suppression, premature ovarian insufficiency, or hyperprolactinemia — leads to bone mineral density loss, cardiovascular risk, and genitourinary atrophy if uncorrected for more than 6-12 months.

typesClassification.description: Failure to achieve menarche by age 15 (or age 13 without breast development). Causes include chromosomal abnormalities (Turner syndrome, 45,X), androgen insensitivity syndrome, congenital absence of the uterus (Müllerian agenesis), imperforate hymen, and constitutional delay.

The most common pathological category (up to 35% of cases). Hypothalamic GnRH secretion is suppressed by low energy availability, high physical stress, psychological stress, or significant weight loss. The female athlete triad — low energy availability, menstrual dysfunction, and low bone density — is the paradigm case.
Hyperprolactinemia from a prolactinoma or drug-induced suppresses GnRH-LH pulsatility. Sheehan syndrome (postpartum pituitary infarction) and empty sella syndrome are less common pituitary causes.

Primary ovarian insufficiency (POI), formerly premature ovarian failure, affects 1% of women under 40 and is characterized by elevated FSH (above 25 IU/L) and low estradiol with loss of follicular reserve. PCOS is the most common ovarian cause and is associated with irregular rather than complete amenorrhea.

Intrauterine adhesions from curettage, uterine surgery, or endometrial infection block menstrual outflow. The ovarian cycle continues normally; estrogen and FSH are normal. Hysteroscopy confirms and treats the adhesions.

primarySymptoms: Absence of menstrual bleeding for 3 or more consecutive months in a previously menstruating woman, or failure to begin periods by age 15 — the defining symptom requiring investigation.

Hot flushes, night sweats, and vaginal dryness in women with amenorrhea due to estrogen deficiency from ovarian insufficiency or hypothalamic suppression — symptoms identical to menopause in affected young women.

Milky nipple discharge (galactorrhea) occurring spontaneously or on expression, indicating hyperprolactinemia from a pituitary adenoma or drug exposure, present in up to 30% of women with prolactinoma-related amenorrhea.
Weight loss, low BMI below 18 kg/m², and fatigue indicating hypothalamic amenorrhea from inadequate caloric intake — the body suppresses reproduction during perceived starvation.
Headache and visual field defects (bitemporal hemianopia) from a large prolactinoma or other sellar mass compressing the optic chiasm — an ophthalmological and neurosurgical emergency.
Hirsutism, acne, and male-pattern hair thinning in women with elevated androgens from PCOS or non-classical congenital adrenal hyperplasia.
Virilization — deepened voice, clitoromegaly, significant muscle mass increase — indicating markedly elevated androgens from an adrenal or ovarian androgen-secreting tumor.
Cyclical lower abdominal pain without visible bleeding in a girl with primary amenorrhea, suggesting a patent ovarian cycle obstructed by an imperforate hymen or transverse vaginal septum.
Bone pain, stress fractures, or recurrent fractures in athletes or underweight women with hypothalamic amenorrhea and estrogen-deficiency bone loss.
Short stature, webbed neck, and widely spaced nipples suggesting Turner syndrome (45,X) as the cause of primary amenorrhea.
earlyWarningSigns: Oligomenorrhea — cycle length extending beyond 35 days — often precedes complete secondary amenorrhea and should prompt evaluation rather than waiting for periods to stop entirely
Significant unintentional or intentional weight loss (more than 10% of body weight) in a girl or woman of reproductive age — hypothalamic suppression of GnRH can precede period loss by weeks
New prescription of antipsychotics, metoclopramide, or antihypertensives known to elevate prolactin in a woman noting missed or irregular periods
Failure of breast development by age 13 or pubic hair development by age 14 — indicating delayed puberty warranting evaluation for chromosomal or endocrine cause
emergencySigns: Severe headache, sudden visual loss, or bitemporal visual field defect in a woman with known or suspected prolactinoma — pituitary apoplexy requires emergency MRI and neurosurgical evaluation
Cyclical severe lower abdominal pain with a palpable lower abdominal mass in a teenager without menarche — suggests hematocolpos from imperforate hymen; requires urgent gynecological drainage
Acute adrenal insufficiency (hypotension, vomiting, confusion) in a woman after postpartum hemorrhage with amenorrhea — Sheehan syndrome with cortisol deficiency needs emergency steroid replacement

causes.description: The most common cause of secondary amenorrhea worldwide. Serum beta-hCG rises before the missed period. Must be excluded with a sensitive urine or serum pregnancy test before any further investigation regardless of stated sexual activity.

causes.cause: Functional hypothalamic amenorrhea (energy deficiency, stress, exercise)
causes.description: Reduced GnRH pulse frequency from the hypothalamus due to low energy availability — caloric restriction, excessive exercise, or psychological stress. LH, FSH, and estradiol fall; prolactin and TSH are normal. Accounts for approximately 35% of secondary amenorrhea in young women. Reverses with weight restoration or reduced exercise stress.

Elevated prolactin suppresses GnRH-LH pulsatility. Causes include prolactinoma (most common pituitary adenoma), dopamine-blocking drugs (antipsychotics, metoclopramide, domperidone), hypothyroidism (TRH stimulates prolactin), and large non-prolactin-secreting pituitary masses causing pituitary stalk compression. Serum prolactin above 25 ng/mL warrants investigation.

Premature depletion or dysfunction of ovarian follicles before age 40. FSH is elevated (above 25 IU/L on two samples 4 weeks apart) and estradiol is low. Causes include chromosome abnormalities (Turner syndrome, fragile X premutation), autoimmune oophoritis (often with Addison's disease), and iatrogenic damage from chemotherapy or radiation.

Most common endocrine disorder of reproductive-age women; affects 5-10% of this group. Elevated LH:FSH ratio, hyperandrogenism, and insulin resistance disrupt normal follicular development and ovulation. Presents more commonly with oligomenorrhea than complete amenorrhea; associated with hirsutism, acne, and obesity.

Endometrial scarring from vigorous curettage (typically post-abortion or postpartum), pelvic tuberculosis, or hysteroscopic surgery obliterates the endometrial cavity or cervical canal, preventing menstrual outflow. Ovarian function and hormone levels are typically normal; diagnosis by saline infusion sonohysterography or hysteroscopy.

riskFactors.factor: Low body weight (BMI below 18 kg/m²) or rapid weight loss
riskFactors.description: Body fat below a critical threshold — roughly 17-22% depending on height and musculature — is associated with hypothalamic amenorrhea. Weight loss of more than 10-15% of body weight within weeks to months is a strong precipitant. Anorexia nervosa causes amenorrhea in over 80% of affected women.

The female athlete triad (low energy availability, menstrual disturbance, low bone density) affects up to 16% of elite female athletes. Distance runners, gymnasts, rowers, and ballet dancers are at highest risk. Energy expenditure exceeds intake even without deliberate restriction.

riskFactors.factor: Chromosomal abnormality (Turner syndrome, fragile X premutation)
riskFactors.description: Turner syndrome (45,X) causes primary amenorrhea in over 90% of affected girls through gonadal dysgenesis. Fragile X permutation carriers (55-200 CGG repeats in FMR1) have a 20-30% lifetime risk of premature ovarian insufficiency.

Anti-ovarian antibodies occur in about 60% of women with autoimmune POI. Approximately 20% of women with POI have coexistent Addison's disease; thyroid autoimmunity is also common. Systemic lupus erythematosus and myasthenia gravis are associated.

riskFactors.factor: Pelvic or brain irradiation and gonadotoxic chemotherapy
riskFactors.description: Radiation to the ovaries (as low as 2 Gy can impair ovarian function) and alkylating agents (cyclophosphamide, busulfan) cause permanent follicular depletion in dose-dependent fashion. Age at treatment is a strong modifier — prepubertal girls tolerate higher doses than adults.

Asherman syndrome develops in 2-40% of women after uterine curettage depending on aggressiveness of the procedure. Risk is higher with repeated curettage for retained products and in women with coexistent endometritis.

Antipsychotics (risperidone, haloperidol), metoclopramide, domperidone, and some antidepressants elevate prolactin. Drug-induced hyperprolactinemia is the most common reversible drug-related cause of amenorrhea and galactorrhea.

affectedDemographics: Adolescent girls aged 13-17 with primary amenorrhea — most commonly due to constitutional delay, Müllerian anomalies, chromosomal disorders, or hypothalamic suppression from anorexia.
Women aged 18-35 with secondary amenorrhea — functional hypothalamic amenorrhea from low weight, intense exercise, or psychological stress accounts for the largest proportion in this age group.
Athletes and ballet dancers — up to 16% of elite female athletes have menstrual disturbance; amenorrhea is most common in those with the lowest energy availability.
Women aged 30-40 with premature ovarian insufficiency — affects 1% of women under 40, with higher rates in those with fragile X premutation or prior ovarian surgery or chemotherapy.
diagnosisOverview: The evaluation of amenorrhea follows a structured algorithm. Pregnancy must be excluded first with serum beta-hCG in all reproductive-age women, regardless of history. After pregnancy is excluded, a targeted history covers: exercise habits, weight changes, psychological stressors, medication use, past pelvic surgeries, galactorrhea, hot flushes, and family history of chromosomal abnormalities or early menopause. Initial blood tests include TSH and free T4 (thyroid dysfunction), prolactin, LH, FSH, and estradiol. Elevated prolactin (above 25 ng/mL) warrants pituitary MRI. Elevated FSH (above 25 IU/L on two samples 4 weeks apart) with low estradiol confirms primary ovarian insufficiency; karyotype is sent to exclude Turner syndrome and FMR1 premutation testing is offered. Low or normal FSH and LH with low estradiol and a clinical picture of weight loss or excess exercise confirms functional hypothalamic amenorrhea. A progesterone challenge test — 10 days of medroxyprogesterone acetate followed by expected withdrawal bleed — is no longer routinely recommended in most guidelines because results are inconsistent, but it can indicate whether significant estrogen priming of the endometrium has occurred. If estrogen and gonadotropins are normal and no cause is apparent, intrauterine adhesions (Asherman syndrome) should be excluded by saline infusion sonohysterography or office hysteroscopy, particularly in women with a history of curettage.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First investigation before any other test; excludes pregnancy as the cause of missed periods
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood is drawn at the same time as other initial tests. Serum hCG is detectable 8-11 days after conception, before a period is missed. Result within 2-4 hours in most labs. Urine hCG dipstick tests are equally reliable in outpatient settings.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes hypothyroidism, which suppresses GnRH through elevated TRH and raises prolactin, causing amenorrhea
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood draw; results in 2-4 hours. Hypothyroidism causing amenorrhea usually shows TSH above 5 mIU/L; correction of thyroid status typically restores menses within 3-6 months.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for hyperprolactinemia from a prolactinoma or drug effect; elevated prolactin suppresses LH pulsatility and estrogen production
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood drawn at rest, at least 1 hour after waking (morning stress elevates prolactin transiently). Single elevated result above 25 ng/mL should be confirmed fasting. Macroprolactin is excluded by polyethylene glycol precipitation in equivocal cases. Pituitary MRI is ordered if prolactin exceeds 100 ng/mL.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes ovarian failure (high FSH, low estradiol) from hypothalamic-pituitary suppression (low-normal FSH, low estradiol) and from PCOS (elevated LH:FSH ratio, normal or high estradiol)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood draw in the early follicular phase (day 2-5 of any cycle or at any time in amenorrhea). FSH above 25 IU/L on two samples 4 weeks apart confirms primary ovarian insufficiency. Low or normal FSH with low estradiol points to central (hypothalamic or pituitary) cause.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates ovarian morphology — polycystic ovaries in PCOS, streak gonads in Turner syndrome — and uterine anatomy for congenital absence or Asherman syndrome
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Transabdominal or transvaginal scan lasting 15-20 minutes. No radiation. Polycystic ovary morphology requires at least 20 follicles per ovary or ovarian volume above 10 mL by Rotterdam criteria (2023 ESHRE/ASRM revision).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes Turner syndrome (45,X) or other chromosomal causes of primary amenorrhea or premature ovarian insufficiency
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw for lymphocyte culture; results take 2-3 weeks. Offered to all women with primary amenorrhea and to those with secondary amenorrhea and elevated FSH under age 35. FMR1 premutation testing is added if POI is confirmed.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is directed at the underlying cause and at managing estrogen deficiency, which drives most of the long-term morbidity. Functional hypothalamic amenorrhea — the most common reversible cause — responds to weight restoration and reduction of exercise to appropriate levels; restoring energy availability reverses GnRH suppression within weeks to months in 80-90% of women. Hyperprolactinemia from a prolactinoma is treated with dopamine agonists (cabergoline or bromocriptine); cabergoline normalizes prolactin in 80-90% of patients and restores menses within 3-6 months. Surgery (transsphenoidal resection) is reserved for dopamine agonist-resistant macroadenomas or those causing persistent visual field defects. Drug-induced hyperprolactinemia is managed by switching to a lower-prolactin drug when clinically possible, in consultation with the prescribing physician. Primary ovarian insufficiency has no treatment to restore ovarian function, but hormone replacement therapy — estradiol plus cyclical progestogen — is recommended by NICE NG23 and ESHRE 2016 guidelines until the natural age of menopause (approximately 51 years) to prevent bone loss and cardiovascular disease; estrogen doses appropriate for POI are higher than standard post-menopausal HRT doses. PCOS-related amenorrhea is managed with lifestyle modification (5-10% weight loss restores ovulation in 50-60% of overweight women) combined with combined oral contraceptive pills or cyclical progestogen to induce regular withdrawal bleeds and protect the endometrium from unopposed estrogen-driven hyperplasia. Asherman syndrome is treated hysteroscopically by lysis of adhesions, followed by estrogen to promote endometrial regeneration.

medicalTreatments.name: Cabergoline (0.25-1 mg twice weekly) for hyperprolactinemia
medicalTreatments.description: Dopamine-D2 agonist that suppresses prolactin secretion and, in prolactinoma, causes tumor shrinkage in 60-80% of patients. Preferred over bromocriptine for better tolerability and once-weekly to twice-weekly dosing. Treatment is continued for at least 2 years; a slow taper is attempted after tumor shrinkage.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Normalizes prolactin in 80-90% of microprolactinoma patients; menses resume in 70-85% within 3-6 months (Colao et al., NEJM 1994 and subsequent meta-analyses)

medicalTreatments.name: Hormone replacement therapy (estradiol + cyclical progestogen) for POI
medicalTreatments.description: Transdermal or oral estradiol 17-beta combined with 12-14 days per month of micronized progesterone or norethisterone. Recommended until age 51 by ESHRE POI Guidelines 2016 and NICE NG23 to prevent osteoporosis, cardiovascular disease, and urogenital atrophy. Does not restore fertility.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents bone loss — women on HRT maintain baseline BMD; those untreated lose 2-5% per year; cardiovascular risk normalization inferred from population data (ESHRE 2016)

medicalTreatments.name: Combined oral contraceptive pill or cyclical progestogen for PCOS amenorrhea
medicalTreatments.description: Provides regular withdrawal bleeds, reduces androgen levels, treats hirsutism and acne, and protects the endometrium from hyperplasia in anovulatory PCOS. Not appropriate in women seeking pregnancy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Induces regular withdrawal bleeding in over 90% of users; endometrial hyperplasia prevention requires a progestogen phase of at least 12 days every 3 months

medicalTreatments.name: Levothyroxine for hypothyroidism-related amenorrhea
medicalTreatments.description: Correction of hypothyroidism normalizes TRH (which had been stimulating prolactin), reverses TSH-driven prolactin elevation, and restores HPO axis function. Dose titrated to normalize TSH into the 0.5-2.5 mIU/L range.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Menses restore in 80-90% of women with hypothyroidism once euthyroidism is achieved, typically within 3-6 months

medicalTreatments.name: Nutritional rehabilitation and energy balance restoration for hypothalamic amenorrhea
medicalTreatments.description: Increasing caloric intake to achieve positive energy balance, reducing exercise load, and treating underlying eating disorders restores hypothalamic GnRH pulsatility. BMI restoration to above 18.5 kg/m² and reduction of training volume by 20-30% are the core interventions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Spontaneous return of ovulation in 80-90% of women with functional hypothalamic amenorrhea who achieve full weight restoration (Gordon et al. Fertil Steril 2017)

medicalTreatments.name: Hysteroscopic adhesiolysis for Asherman syndrome
medicalTreatments.description: Operative hysteroscopy divides intrauterine adhesions using scissors, electrocautery, or laser under direct vision. Post-procedure estrogen (usually oral estradiol 4 mg daily for 6-8 weeks with add-back progestogen) promotes endometrial regeneration.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Restoration of menstruation in 75-90% of cases; live birth rates after hysteroscopic adhesiolysis range from 38-80% depending on severity of adhesions (AAGL guidelines 2022)

surgicalOptions.name: Transsphenoidal pituitary surgery for prolactinoma
surgicalOptions.description: Endoscopic or microscopic resection of a prolactin-secreting adenoma via the nasal sphenoid sinus. Reserved for macroadenomas causing visual field defects, dopamine agonist intolerance, or resistance after adequate drug trial.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Biochemical cure in 70-90% of microprolactinomas; 40-60% for macroadenomas; recurrence in 20-30% at 10 years

surgicalOptions.name: Hysteroscopic adhesiolysis for Asherman syndrome
surgicalOptions.description: Operative hysteroscopy under direct vision restores the uterine cavity. Adhesion barriers (oxidized cellulose, hyaluronic acid gel) and intrauterine devices or balloon catheters are sometimes placed post-procedure to prevent re-adhesion.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Menstruation restored in 75-90%; fertility outcomes depend on pre-operative adhesion severity

surgicalOptions.name: Imperforate hymen or vaginal septum correction
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical incision of an imperforate hymen or resection of a transverse vaginal septum under general or spinal anaesthesia in adolescents with primary amenorrhea and cryptomenorrhea (cyclical pelvic pain without visible bleeding). Single procedure in most cases.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Near 100% resolution of outflow obstruction when diagnosis is made before pelvic organ damage from hematometrocolpos occurs

alternativeTreatments.name: Cognitive behavioral therapy and psychological support for eating disorder-related hypothalamic amenorrhea
alternativeTreatments.description: Multidisciplinary treatment including CBT and nutritional counseling for anorexia nervosa or restrictive eating is the only evidence-based approach to restoring energy balance and reversing hypothalamic amenorrhea in this population. Pharmacologic ovulation induction without weight restoration is ineffective and unsafe.

whySeeSpecialist: A reproductive endocrinologist or gynecologist should be involved when amenorrhea persists beyond 3 months, when pregnancy is desired, or when a specific diagnosis (POI, prolactinoma, Asherman syndrome) requires specialist management. Adolescents with primary amenorrhea should be evaluated by age 15 to identify chromosomal and anatomical abnormalities that benefit from early treatment.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a gynecologist or reproductive endocrinologist with access to pituitary MRI, hormone laboratory services, and hysteroscopy. For eating disorder-related hypothalamic amenorrhea, a multidisciplinary team including a dietitian and mental health clinician is essential. Fertility clinics are best suited for women with POI or Asherman syndrome who are planning conception.
hospitalCriteria: Pituitary apoplexy — sudden severe headache with visual loss in a woman with known prolactinoma
Hematocolpos or hematometrocolpos from outflow obstruction in a teenager with primary amenorrhea and cyclical pain
Acute adrenal crisis from undiagnosed Sheehan syndrome or autoimmune adrenal insufficiency co-occurring with POI
Admission for nutritional rehabilitation in severe anorexia nervosa with BMI below 14 kg/m²
keyFacilities: Pituitary tumour centres with endoscopic neurosurgery
preventionStrategies: Maintain a healthy weight and adequate caloric intake relative to exercise; energy availability above 30 kcal/kg lean body mass per day prevents hypothalamic suppression in athletes.
Reduce training load if menstrual irregularity begins — oligomenorrhea and amenorrhea are reversible at this early stage without bone loss.
Seek prompt treatment for eating disorders before amenorrhea leads to irreversible bone loss; bone accrual is maximal in the teenage years.
Screen women with POI risk factors — family history, fragile X premutation, autoimmune disease, prior chemotherapy — with baseline FSH and estradiol so estrogen replacement is started early.
Minimize aggressive uterine curettage during management of pregnancy loss; suction aspiration is preferred over sharp curettage to reduce Asherman syndrome risk.
Review and switch prolactin-elevating medications with the prescribing physician before amenorrhea becomes established when alternatives are available.
lifestyleModifications: Achieve and maintain a BMI of at least 18.5 kg/m² through supervised nutritional rehabilitation if amenorrhea is related to low body weight or eating disorder.
Reduce exercise volume by at least 20-30% if high-intensity training is suspected as the trigger; consider a sports medicine or exercise physiology assessment.
Take weight-bearing exercise (walking, resistance training) to protect bone mineral density while estrogen levels are low, in addition to HRT.
Adhere to hormone replacement or oral contraceptive prescriptions to prevent bone loss while underlying causes are being addressed.
Attend regular gynecological follow-up for endometrial surveillance when amenorrhea is associated with PCOS and elevated estrogen without cyclical progestogen.
Practice stress-reduction strategies (CBT, mindfulness) where psychological stress is contributing to hypothalamic suppression.

recommended: Calorie-dense, nutrient-rich foods (nuts, avocados, olive oil, lean protein, whole grains) to correct energy deficiency in athletes and women with restrictive eating
Calcium (1,000-1,200 mg daily) from dairy, fortified foods, or supplements to counteract estrogen-deficiency bone loss
Vitamin D (800-2,000 IU daily) to support calcium absorption and maintain bone mineral density
Iron-rich foods (red meat, legumes, spinach with vitamin C) to address the iron deficiency common in athletic women with previously heavy periods
avoid: Extreme caloric restriction or elimination diets that reduce energy availability below 30 kcal/kg lean body mass per day
Excessive caffeine, which may suppress appetite and interfere with calcium absorption at intakes above 400 mg/day
Very high-fibre, low-fat diets that provide inadequate energy density for the demands of athletic training
Alcohol in excess of moderate intake, which impairs pituitary LH secretion and may worsen bone metabolism

exerciseGuidelines: For women with hypothalamic amenorrhea related to exercise, the goal is to reduce training intensity or volume until menstruation resumes. A sports dietitian and exercise physiologist should calculate energy availability and identify the deficit. Once menses return, a graduated return to training with monitoring of menstrual regularity is appropriate. Weight-bearing exercise (walking, resistance training) is specifically recommended for women with bone loss from any cause of amenorrhea.
dailyManagement: Take hormone replacement therapy or oral contraceptive pill at the same time each day to maintain consistent hormone levels and protect the endometrium.
Track any bleeding — light spotting or withdrawal bleed timing — to monitor treatment response.
Perform a monthly self-check of weight and energy balance if hypothalamic amenorrhea is the diagnosis; notify the care team if weight falls below target.
Perform annual DEXA bone density scans when amenorrhea has been present for more than 12 months without adequate estrogen replacement.
Report any new headache, visual change, or galactorrhea to the treating physician, as these may indicate prolactinoma growth.
Complete all follow-up hormone testing as scheduled — FSH, estradiol, and prolactin at 3-6 month intervals while treatment is being adjusted.
prognosis: Prognosis depends on the underlying cause. Functional hypothalamic amenorrhea from low energy availability has an excellent prognosis — over 80% of women resume spontaneous ovulation within 6 months of restoring appropriate energy balance and reducing exercise stress. Drug-induced hyperprolactinemia reverses in nearly all women when the offending drug is withdrawn or switched. Prolactinoma-related amenorrhea responds to cabergoline in 80-90% of cases, with menses resuming within 3-6 months. Primary ovarian insufficiency is not reversible — the underlying follicular depletion does not respond to treatment — but hormone replacement prevents bone loss and cardiovascular disease, and spontaneous pregnancy occurs in 5-10% of women with POI over time. Asherman syndrome after hysteroscopic lysis carries live birth rates of 38-80% depending on adhesion severity before surgery. PCOS-related amenorrhea is managed long-term; reproductive outcomes with ovulation induction are generally favorable. Untreated amenorrhea of any cause lasting more than 12 months results in measurable bone mineral density loss (1-2% per year) and increased long-term fracture risk.
recoveryTimeline: Functional hypothalamic amenorrhea: ovulation may resume within 4-12 weeks of adequate energy restoration. Cabergoline for prolactinoma: prolactin normalizes within 4-8 weeks, menses resume within 3-6 months. Thyroid correction: menses resume within 3-6 months. PCOS with weight loss: ovulation typically resumes within 3-6 months of 5-10% body weight reduction. Asherman surgery: first menses expected within 6-8 weeks of procedure with estrogen support.
complications: Osteoporosis and stress fractures — estrogen deficiency reduces bone mineral density by 1-2% per year; fracture risk doubles after 5-10 years of untreated amenorrhea without HRT.
Infertility — anovulation from any cause of amenorrhea prevents natural conception; assisted reproductive technology may be needed for PCOS, POI, or residual hypothalamic dysfunction.

Endometrial hyperplasia and endometrial carcinoma — in PCOS-related amenorrhea, unopposed estrogen stimulation of the endometrium without cyclical progestogen increases long-term cancer risk 3-4 fold.
Cardiovascular disease — prolonged estrogen deficiency in young women with POI doubles the relative risk of coronary artery disease and stroke compared with age-matched controls.
Pituitary apoplexy — hemorrhage or infarction into a prolactinoma causes sudden severe headache, visual loss, and potentially life-threatening cortisol deficiency.

Psychological morbidity — amenorrhea is associated with body image disturbance, anxiety about fertility, and reduced quality of life; untreated eating disorders that cause amenorrhea carry substantial mortality.

supportResources.name: The Daisy Network — Premature Ovarian Insufficiency Support
supportResources.description: UK charity providing peer support, information, and research updates for women with premature ovarian insufficiency.

supportResources.name: The Pituitary Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient information and support for women with prolactinoma and other pituitary causes of amenorrhea.

supportResources.name: ESHRE Patient Version: Premature Ovarian Insufficiency
supportResources.description: European Society of Human Reproduction and Embryology guideline summary for patients on POI diagnosis, HRT, and fertility.

confusedWithConditions.name: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PCOS causes oligomenorrhea (infrequent periods, cycles longer than 35 days) more commonly than complete amenorrhea. FSH is low-normal, LH may be elevated relative to FSH, estradiol is normal or elevated, and androgens are often high. In functional hypothalamic amenorrhea, FSH and LH are both low, estradiol is low, and there is no hyperandrogenism. Pelvic ultrasound and hormone panel reliably distinguish them.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hypothyroidism causes menstrual irregularity (more often heavy irregular periods than amenorrhea) through elevated TRH stimulating prolactin. TSH is elevated; prolactin may be mildly raised. Correction of hypothyroidism with levothyroxine reverses menstrual dysfunction. Amenorrhea from functional hypothalamic suppression has normal TSH and near-normal or low prolactin.

confusedWithConditions.name: Endometriosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Endometriosis causes painful, often heavy periods — the opposite of amenorrhea — and does not typically suppress the HPO axis. However, severe endometriosis treated with GnRH agonists (e.g., leuprolide) induces a temporary medical amenorrhea as part of treatment. The distinction is clinical: endometriosis presents with dysmenorrhea and dyspareunia, not primary menstrual absence.

confusedWithConditions.name: Abnormal Uterine Bleeding
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Abnormal uterine bleeding involves irregular, heavy, prolonged, or intermenstrual bleeding — excess rather than absent menstruation. Some conditions (PCOS, early POI, thyroid disease) can cause either amenorrhea or abnormal bleeding at different stages of disease. Amenorrhea requires an entirely different diagnostic workup focused on the HPO axis.

relatedConditions.name: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Most common single cause of anovulatory oligomenorrhea and secondary amenorrhea; shares diagnostic evaluation and some treatment strategies.

relatedConditions.name: Abnormal Uterine Bleeding
relatedConditions.relevance: The opposing menstrual disorder — understanding the causes of amenorrhea and AUB relies on the same HPO axis framework.

relatedConditions.name: Endometriosis
relatedConditions.relevance: GnRH agonist therapy for endometriosis induces therapeutic amenorrhea; Asherman syndrome from prior endometriosis surgery can cause outflow-tract amenorrhea.

relatedConditions.name: Uterine Fibroids
relatedConditions.relevance: Submucosal fibroids cause heavy irregular bleeding rather than amenorrhea, but their treatment (endometrial ablation, hysterectomy) can cause subsequent amenorrhea.

relatedConditions.name: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
relatedConditions.relevance: Insulin resistance is central to PCOS pathophysiology; many PCOS women have impaired glucose tolerance or type 2 diabetes, and glycaemic management improves menstrual regularity.

faqs.question: What is the most common cause of missed periods?
faqs.answer: Pregnancy is the most common cause of missed periods worldwide and must be excluded with a pregnancy test before any other investigation. After pregnancy, the most common pathological causes are functional hypothalamic amenorrhea (from low weight, over-exercise, or stress), PCOS, hyperprolactinemia, and thyroid disease.

faqs.question: How long without a period is considered amenorrhea?
faqs.answer: Secondary amenorrhea is defined as the absence of periods for 3 or more consecutive months in a woman who has previously menstruated regularly, or 6 or more months in a woman with previously irregular cycles. Primary amenorrhea is defined as no first period by age 15.

faqs.question: Can stress cause amenorrhea?
faqs.answer: Yes. Significant psychological stress suppresses hypothalamic GnRH secretion, reducing LH and FSH and thereby stopping ovulation and menstruation. This is called functional hypothalamic amenorrhea. It is reversible when the stressor is removed and energy balance is restored. Cortisol elevation from chronic stress directly inhibits the HPO axis.

faqs.question: Can too much exercise cause missed periods?
faqs.answer: Yes. The female athlete triad describes the combination of low energy availability, menstrual disturbance, and low bone density in athletes. Even without intentional caloric restriction, energy expenditure from high-intensity training can exceed intake enough to suppress hypothalamic GnRH and stop ovulation. Distance runners, gymnasts, and rowers are most commonly affected.

faqs.question: What blood tests are done for amenorrhea?
faqs.answer: Initial blood tests include serum beta-hCG (pregnancy), TSH and free T4 (thyroid), prolactin, FSH, LH, and estradiol. Depending on results, testosterone and DHEAS are added to screen for PCOS or adrenal disease. FSH above 25 IU/L on two samples 4 weeks apart confirms premature ovarian insufficiency.

faqs.question: Is amenorrhea always a sign of something serious?
faqs.answer: Not always. Pregnancy, breastfeeding, and the transition to menopause are normal physiologic causes. Many women on certain contraceptive methods (hormonal IUDs, implants, depot progestogens) have light or absent periods as a normal effect. However, unexplained amenorrhea lasting 3 or more months warrants medical evaluation to exclude treatable conditions and prevent bone loss.

faqs.question: Can amenorrhea affect bone density?
faqs.answer: Yes, significantly. Estrogen deficiency from any cause of amenorrhea reduces bone mineral density by 1-2% per year. Young women with hypothalamic amenorrhea or primary ovarian insufficiency who are untreated for several years have measurably lower bone density and a higher long-term fracture risk compared with age-matched women with normal estrogen levels.

faqs.question: Can amenorrhea be treated without medication?
faqs.answer: Functional hypothalamic amenorrhea caused by low energy availability or excessive exercise can resolve without medication through weight restoration, caloric increase, or reduced training volume. Approximately 80% of women with this type of amenorrhea resume periods once energy balance is corrected. Other causes — prolactinoma, POI, Asherman syndrome, thyroid disease — require medical or surgical treatment.

faqs.question: Does amenorrhea mean I cannot get pregnant?
faqs.answer: Amenorrhea reflects absent ovulation in most cases, which means natural conception is not occurring. However, fertility can be restored when the underlying cause is treated. Ovulation induction or IVF is possible for many women. Even in premature ovarian insufficiency, spontaneous pregnancy occurs in 5-10% of women over time.

faqs.question: What is primary ovarian insufficiency?
faqs.answer: Primary ovarian insufficiency (POI) is the loss of normal ovarian function before age 40, characterized by elevated FSH above 25 IU/L and low estradiol with absent or infrequent periods. It is not the same as early menopause — 5-10% of women with POI still ovulate intermittently and can conceive. Hormone replacement is recommended until at least age 51.

faqs.question: What is Asherman syndrome?
faqs.answer: Asherman syndrome is scarring inside the uterus that blocks menstrual flow. It usually develops after aggressive uterine curettage (for miscarriage or abortion), endometritis, or uterine surgery. Ovarian function is normal, but blood cannot leave the uterus. Diagnosis is by hysteroscopy; treatment is surgical lysis of adhesions with high success rates.

faqs.question: Why does a prolactinoma cause amenorrhea?
faqs.answer: Prolactin secreted by a prolactinoma directly suppresses GnRH pulsatility in the hypothalamus, reducing LH and FSH and preventing ovulation and estrogen production. The result is amenorrhea and often galactorrhea. Cabergoline, a dopamine agonist, reduces prolactin levels and shrinks the tumor in most cases, restoring menses within 3-6 months.

faqs.question: Is amenorrhea related to PCOS?
faqs.answer: PCOS causes oligomenorrhea (infrequent periods) in most affected women and complete secondary amenorrhea in a smaller proportion. The anovulation in PCOS results from elevated LH, insulin resistance, and androgen excess disrupting follicular development. Weight loss of 5-10% restores ovulation in 50-60% of overweight women with PCOS-related menstrual disturbance.

faqs.question: At what age should a girl be seen for not having her first period?
faqs.answer: A girl should be evaluated if she has no periods by age 15 with otherwise normal pubertal development, or by age 13 if there is no breast development. Primary amenorrhea evaluation seeks chromosomal abnormalities, anatomical malformations, and hormonal causes, most of which are effectively managed when found early.

faqs.question: What is the difference between amenorrhea and oligomenorrhea?
faqs.answer: Oligomenorrhea means infrequent periods — cycles longer than 35 days, fewer than 9 periods per year. Amenorrhea means complete absence of periods for 3 or more months (secondary) or no first period by age 15 (primary). Oligomenorrhea often precedes secondary amenorrhea and shares many of the same underlying causes, particularly PCOS and hypothalamic dysfunction.

symptomKeywords: no period for 3 months not pregnant
hot flushes young woman no period
metaDescription: Amenorrhea is the absence of menstrual periods, with causes from low weight to PCOS and prolactinoma. Diagnosis, treatment, and fertility impact reviewed here.
